item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I genuinelly liked this, until that interview. It just isn't good: the tone is lacking, they way they speak is wooden and seems rehearsed, and some parts even seem to have mistakes. If you could fix it, then it would be an okayish article.

Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wanted to upvote this. I really did. You have a concept that I could very plausibly fall in love with. What you might do to get folks like me to that point is just tighten a few tonal bolts here and there. Passages such as

SCP-2042-1 is a Caucasian male, in its late fifties. The appearance of the subject is otherwise unremarkable, with slight balding and grey hair and beard, but they are lacking a mouth orifice of any sort.

(In what way is a Caucasian male in his late fifties remarkable? Mouth and orifice can be interpreted as the same concept. Perhaps simply leave "mouth" or elaborate to " any facial orifice other than standard human tear ducts and nasal passages." Maintaining that other than being fifty and Caucasian the man is wholly unremarkable and then mentioning mouthlessness defeats the former statement.)

and

SCP-2042-1 appears capable of controlling SCP-2042-2 to a certain degree, with distance and number of "clones" being used making the task more difficult.

(The Foundation would have tested and quantified control and number of clones able to be controlled and in what manner each clone can be controlled, continuing ad nauseam. My suggestion is that you could replace "appears" with "is", and create more defined and only vaguely scientific wording for the latter half of the passage. Something along the lines of ", although variables such as distance from the original instance, quantity of cloned instances, and intensity of activity can hinder SCP-2042-1 in maintaining this control.")

take the reader a second to consider or simply feel weird in a literary sense, dragging the article along. You have a great concept, and, having reviewed your article thoroughly, a great many sticking points which can be utilized to greater effect. As I already said. I could very well love this article. I truly hope to edit this opinion in the future and find that I do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's because the article both refers to SCP-2042-1 as well as the -2's in separate occasions, sometimes referring to them all as a group. -1 is notable from the -2's, of which there are 20.

So usually when referring to a singular thing, "it" is more appropriate than "they". :V

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your pronouns are inconsistent. You refer to the subject as both "it" and "they". "It" is correct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Applause

I like the idea of MC&D branching out into a capella performances. The kind of act for upper-class gatherings.

Now trying to picture 19 voices doing Rockapella. Maybe with two of them beatboxing. Just a stray thought.

+1. Nice, simple, odd. No horror needed.

item.title: Well done.
item.content:encoded: I have a bit of a weakness for the entries that succeed in their weirdness without being creepy, and this is a very quiet, dignified, very odd scip. Thanks for adding it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh, sorry about that. I tend to do that a bit involuntarily, I have a very weird way of wording things. Trying my best to move away from that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good, but the article uses three different pronouns to refer to this guy (it, he, and singular they), sometimes even within the same paragraph. It's driving me up a wall. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've hit +35! Wooo milestones
EDIT: Hit +40! *achievement unlocked: "git a lief u scrub" +20 ScipBucks*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now THAT is compensating. I'm not that good at english though, so I can't help with wording. I think that 'faster metabolism' could be a better concept but not sure how to use it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed the parts about digesting and feeding a bit, see if you like them. Suggestions on better wording are also appreciated.

item.title: Quick sketch time!
item.content:encoded: A quick drawing of SCP-2042-1 with four instances of SCP-2042-2, in the middle of a performance. http://pbs.twimg.com/media/Bu6LkEQIYAAK0vn.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, two things I don't like:

which have been shown to be genetically identical to SCP-2042-1 and each other.

If A = C and B = C then obviously A = B.
If they're all identical to SCP-2042-1, no need to also say they're identical to eachother.

To compensate this, SCP-2042-1's digestive system appears to be 345% more effective.

That's -not- compensating, even if it were possible.
If they STILL need to be given more food, even though they digest 3 times better, it means they normally needs 12 times the ammount of nutrients, and they compensate it digesting 300% better, so they only need 3 times the food. So, in the way you explained it, 'compensating' doesn't work. (ok, it's 3.45 times better, I simplified it to explain)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some comments

+ show block
– hide block

They are to be fed three times as much food as an average build human would normally require…SCP-2042-2 are a series of 20 humanoids, which have been shown to be genetically identical to SCP-2042-1 and each other.

That makes no sense. Why do they need only three times as much food? Also if they are genetically identical to one instance there's no need to specify that they are also still identical to each other.

SCP-2042-1 is a Caucasian male, in its late fifties. The appearance of the subject is otherwise unremarkable, with slight balding and grey hair and beard, but lacks a mouth orifice of any sort.

Yet another Caucasian. You do know there's a significant population of Africans, Afro-Caribbean and African-American ex-pats in France right?

Their muscles are composed of particularly fibrous tissue, resembling plant matter in architecture.

Then they would look literally nothing like muscles. So much so that you couldn't call them muscles. Plant fibers are long, mostly dead strings made up of cellulose that are hollow to facilitate flexibility and water transport. Muscle fibers are long, flexible, contain no cellulose and don't have a passage through the center. A histologist, a doctor or a botanist would never mistake the two.

It is notable that vocalisations made through SCP-2042-2 instances by SCP-2042-1 are in the form of singing in various styles.

^This sentence is awkward.

"SCP-2042-1 communicates entirely in song through SCP-2042-2."

To compensate this, SCP-2042-1's digestive system appears to be 345% more effective.

There's an upper limit on the energy and nutrients you can extract through digestion. I'm not sure if this strange percentage would really be sufficient or plausible.

"La Conférence Gangrène"

The Conference of Brand? That's what Gangrene translates to… "Brand".

Also nobody in France thought it was weird that there were 20 identical men led by a man without a mouth were in a choral group?

Well, one day while I was at my favorite cafe, enjoying my croissant in peace, two men in dark clothing approached me.

"One day I was approached by two suspicious people while eating a highly stereotypical breakfast. I AM FRENCH"

I told them to get the ████ of my face, thinking they were just there to tease an old silent,

No need to blackbox a swear. The Foundation is made up of adults. And "old silent" don't you mean "old mute"? If he used a french colloquialism the Foundation would translate it to mean what he meant. They wouldn't use a literal thing in the transcript. If he's fluent in English he wouldn't mistake the two words.

Other than that? This skip could work.

item.title: Re: So far so good
item.content:encoded: Ah, thank ye kindly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Instead of posting multiple comments in succession, edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup, and it's in the rules. :)

item.title: Re: So far so good
item.content:encoded: Instead of replying to your own comments, edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup, and it's in the rules. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty good. Well done, Nanoro.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2: No, the "clones" lack all other features except a mouth: moles, a navel, reproductive organs etc. in other words anything that would differentiate them from another clone.
4: No. SCP-2042-1 simply uses the clones as his mouths. The food is not exactly fed to them, but him. I can see that it is a tad confusing of a sentence though.
5: .. Yes, it is. Didn't even notice until you pointed that out. :l
6: Try being a mute all your life, not being able to say a word while the world around you constantly speaks their mind. I know I wouldn't be in too high spirits about that.

As for the rest, my wording can be a bit off, yes… It's a thing I'm furiously working on. Thank you for the critique, it was helpful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

The appearance of the subject is otherwise unremarkable, with slight balding and grey hair and beard, but lacks a mouth orifice of any sort.

The appearance […] lacks a mouth.

The subject can lack a mouth. The appearance of the subject can't lack a mouth.

Aside from a mouth, all instances lack any other distinguishing features.

I think what you mean is facial features. Absence of facial features would in fact be a distinguishing feature.

Vocalising appears to be the only task all instances are capable of despite the full number of them being used simultaneously.

This sentence isn't very clear. I get what you're saying, I think, but it could be worded better.

They are to be fed three times as much food as an average build human would normally require.

SCP-2042-1 requires approximately three times as much sustenance as an average male of his age and build.

Since -1 receives all the food consumed by -2s, and -2s are fed three times as much as normal humans, and there's 20 -2s, then -1 actually requires 60 times as much sustenance as an average human, not three times.

MTF-Gamma-769 (My Little Friend)

SCP-2042-2-1: Oh, my little friends?

This is a weird coincidence.

living in a world of silence and despair.

That seems overly melodramatic.

Intention to off me, if you understand what I mean by that.

Seriously? Because he's mute he must be suicidal? Meh.

Addendum: It has been requested that SCP-2042 is allowed to perform at Site-██. It has gained quite a reputation amongst researchers preceding containment, and as such would greatly boost the morale. Approval is pending.

I don't like this and don't see how it adds anything to the article.

Other than that, not bad.

item.title: Re: So far so good
item.content:encoded: *successful

item.title: So far so good
item.content:encoded: It's been up for a day and already at +21. I must admit, this is far more successful than I expected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They are: it was suggested to me that since the English and French phrasings are quite different, the translation should sound a bit strange.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this works as a good humanoid skip. Admittedly, my first thought was "Is this Pikmin?" I'm really not sure why. There are a few odd word choices in the interview and I'm wondering if those are intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. It's simple and well written, and a good version of the Wish-gone-bad trope, without going overboard.

I might shave off the addendum, and un-redact some of the redactions (like the digestion % and possibly the name of the singing group), but as it is right now, it's good.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ((Also the latter is a proposal in pending, not approved of.))

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, thanks for the feedback anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's some odd redactions in this one. Why is the specific efficiency of the skip's digestive system censored (assuming that something like that is as precisely measurable as to yield a percentage)? And curse words are generally not blackboxed.

As for the concept, it's not bad, but I'm not particularly grabbed by it. The weird little extraneous bits, like the shoe-horned MC&D reference and the fact that it's allowed to perform for staff as a morale-boosting measure, drag this into downvote territory for me.

item.title: Re: Trivia
item.content:encoded: Well, the old English books I've learned my English from are to blame.

item.title: Trivia
item.content:encoded:
Actually, that was the correct spelling at one time. See the second definition. It was influenced by the Greek khorde, which referred to the biological structure known as tripe, and thus also the gut-based strings of the lyre.

You're right, of course; that spelling fell out of fashion some time ago. But it was correct at one time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
vocal chords

No. There is no definition of "chord" that would make sense here, especially considering you're talking about cords of muscle. Vocal cords.

This comment is also for everyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have to put a space after the > for it to format correctly. I feex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried that, didn't work… Please do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All it really needs is a "> " between the lines. Want me to fix it for you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, it is a nod towards MC&D. I reckon they wanted one heck of an opera show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2042-2-1: … I… Believe that there was a Mr. Cartier and D'arc. Wonder if the other was related to that D'arc.

Mr. Cartier and D'arc. Carter and Dark. Is this an MC&D Scip?

Either way, Upvote from me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lemme see what I can do about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. A mute and the choir. Upvote from me.

but

SCP-2042-2-1: … I… Believe that there was a Mr. Cartier and D'arc. Wonder if the other was related to that D'arc.
Dr. N█████: … I believe that is highly unlikely.

I believe there should be a line space between those.

item.title: Let there be light
item.content:encoded: Well, here goes nothing. First attempt is first. Please be gentle c;
